**Du gamla, du fria**[[a]](#cite_note-5) (lit. 'You ancient, you free'),[[5]](#cite_note-6)[[6]](#cite_note-7) is the *[de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto")* [national anthem](/wiki/National_anthem "National anthem") of [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden").[[7]](#cite_note-:4-8) Originally titled **Sång till Norden**[[b]](#cite_note-9) (lit. 'Song to the Nordics'), its lyrics were written in 1844 by Swedish antiquarian [Richard Dybeck](/wiki/Richard_Dybeck "Richard Dybeck"), who set them to a variation of the old [folk melody](/wiki/Swedish_folk_music "Swedish folk music") *Kärestans död* (lit. 'Death of a loved one')*.*[[8]](#cite_note-:0-10)[[9]](#cite_note-11)

The song has been widely recognised as the national anthem since the late 19th century, gaining prominence after [King Oscar II](/wiki/Oscar_II "Oscar II"), attending a dinner in 1893, stood in acknowledgement upon hearing the song.[[10]](#cite_note-:1-12)[[8]](#cite_note-:0-10) It gained further recognition in 1938, when [Sveriges Radio](/wiki/Sveriges_Radio "Sveriges Radio"), the national public broadcaster, began playing it at the conclusion of its daily programming.[[8]](#cite_note-:0-10) In 2000, the [Riksdag](/wiki/Riksdag "Riksdag") (Swedish Parliament) declined a proposal to officially recognise *Du gamla, du fria* as the national anthem, stating that its established status through tradition rendered formal adoption unnecessary.[[11]](#cite_note-:2-13)

Dybeck's original lyrics consist of two verses, reflecting the ideals of [Scandinavianism](/wiki/Scandinavism "Scandinavism").[[12]](#cite_note-14) It references the [Nordic region](/wiki/Nordic_countries "Nordic countries") ([Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"): *Norden*) but not Sweden itself, making the [Polish national anthem](/wiki/Poland_Is_Not_Yet_Lost "Poland Is Not Yet Lost") the only national anthem to reference Sweden.[[13]](#cite_note-15) Various attempts have been made to introduce additional verses that explicitly mention Sweden, but none have been popularly adopted.[[14]](#cite_note-16)

In addition to the national anthem, Sweden has a [royal anthem](/wiki/Royal_anthem "Royal anthem"), [Kungssången](/wiki/Kungss%C3%A5ngen "Kungssången") (lit. 'The King's Song').[[15]](#cite_note-17)[[16]](#cite_note-18)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Du_gamla,_du_fria&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The original lyrics were written by [Richard Dybeck](/wiki/Richard_Dybeck "Richard Dybeck") in 1844, to the melody of a variant of the ballad "Kärestans död" (lit. 'Death of a loved one'). The ballad type is classified as D 280 in [The Types of the Scandinavian Medieval Ballad](/wiki/The_Types_of_the_Scandinavian_Medieval_Ballad "The Types of the Scandinavian Medieval Ballad"); the variant from [Västmanland](/wiki/V%C3%A4stmanland "Västmanland") that Dybeck reproduced is classified as [SMB](/wiki/Sveriges_Medeltida_Ballader "Sveriges Medeltida Ballader") 133 G.[[17]](#cite_note-19) It was recorded by Rosa Wretman at the beginning of the 1840s. Dybeck published the traditional text in *Folk-lore I*, and the melody in 1845 in his *Runa*, where he also published his new text "Sång till Norden" (Song for/to the North).

Dybeck himself originally wrote the beginning as "Du gamla, du friska" (lit. 'You ancient, you healthy'), but in the late 1850s, he changed the lyrics to "Du gamla, du fria". The song was already published in several song books and sung with "Du gamla, du friska", but a priest who had known Dybeck took the opportunity to inform the singer most associated with the song, opera singer [Carl Fredrik Lundqvist](/w/index.php?title=Carl_Fredrik_Lundqvist&action=edit&redlink=1 "Carl Fredrik Lundqvist (page does not exist)") [[sv](https://sv.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carl_Fredrik_Lundqvist "sv:Carl Fredrik Lundqvist")], about the change in the year 1900. From that point on, printings of the "friska" version ceased to be seen in song books, but a recording from 1905 where it is sung with "friska" still exists.[[18]](#cite_note-20) The Swedish composer [Edvin Kallstenius](/wiki/Edvin_Kallstenius "Edvin Kallstenius") made an orchestral arrangement of the song in 1933.[[4]](#cite_note-Holm-4)

By the early 20th century, many[*[who?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] regarded the song unsuitable as a national anthem. From the 1890s, it was included in the "patriotic songs" section of song books, but up to the 1920s it was occasionally published just as "folk music". In 1899, a contest to produce a national anthem was held. It led to [Verner von Heidenstam](/wiki/Verner_von_Heidenstam "Verner von Heidenstam") writing "Sverige", but did not lead to a new national anthem.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReinhammar2013152-21)

Patriotic sentiment is notably absent from the text of the original two verses, due to their being written in the spirit of [Scandinavism](/wiki/Scandinavism "Scandinavism") popular at the time.[[c]](#cite_note-22) After the song started to acquire its informal status as the national anthem, various people wrote additional verses to increase the "Swedish-ness" of the song. The aforementioned Lundqvist wrote his own third verse beginning with "Jag älskar dig Sverige" (lit. 'Thee I Adore, Sweden'); [Frans Österblom](/w/index.php?title=Frans_%C3%96sterblom&action=edit&redlink=1 "Frans Österblom (page does not exist)") [[sv](https://sv.wikipedia.org/wiki/Frans_%C3%96sterblom "sv:Frans Österblom")] wrote four verses beginning with "Jag älskar min hembygd" (lit. 'I Adore My Homestead'); and [Louise Ahlén](/wiki/Louise_Ahl%C3%A9n_(songwriter) "Louise Ahlén (songwriter)") with two verses. However, these are not accepted as part of the anthem, and are not normally published or sung.

## Status

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Du_gamla,_du_fria&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Status")]

Similarly to the national anthems of [Finland](/wiki/Maamme "Maamme") and the [United Kingdom](/wiki/God_Save_the_King "God Save the King"),[[20]](#cite_note-23) *Du gamla, du fria* is not defined in Sweden's constitutional [Basic Laws](/wiki/Basic_Laws_of_Sweden "Basic Laws of Sweden"), nor has it been formally recognised by other legislation, unlike Sweden's [flag](/wiki/Flag_of_Sweden "Flag of Sweden")[[21]](#cite_note-24) and [coat of arms](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_Sweden "Coat of arms of Sweden").[[22]](#cite_note-25) Despite this, the song enjoys universal recognition, and is used at official events such as [state visits](/wiki/State_visit "State visit") and the [Opening of the Riksdag](/wiki/Opening_of_the_Riksdag "Opening of the Riksdag"),[[23]](#cite_note-26) at sporting events, and at the [Nobel Prize](/wiki/Nobel_Prize "Nobel Prize") Award Ceremony.[[24]](#cite_note-27)

The song gained status as the national anthem from the late 19th century, becoming predominant in 1893 after [King Oscar II](/wiki/Oscar_II "Oscar II") stood up in acknowledgement of the song at a dinner at [Lund University](/wiki/Lund_University "Lund University").[[10]](#cite_note-:1-12)[[8]](#cite_note-:0-10) In 1938, [Sveriges Radio](/wiki/Sveriges_Radio "Sveriges Radio"), the national public radio broadcasting service, began playing it at the conclusion of its daily programming, gaining it further recognition.[[8]](#cite_note-:0-10) By the end of the 1930s, the song's status had effectively been established.[[25]](#cite_note-28)

The song's status has been the subject of multiple motions in the [Riksdag](/wiki/Riksdag "Riksdag"). In 1986, a motion proposed granting the song legal recognition, but it was not passed.[[26]](#cite_note-29) Further motions from [MPs](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament_(Sweden) "Member of Parliament (Sweden)") of various [political parties](/wiki/List_of_political_parties_in_Sweden "List of political parties in Sweden") in 2000,[[27]](#cite_note-30) 2007,[[28]](#cite_note-31) 2019[[29]](#cite_note-32) and 2024[[30]](#cite_note-33) have also sought official status for the anthem. However, none of these proposals have ever been accepted by the Riksdag.[[11]](#cite_note-:2-13)

In 2000, a Riksdag committee concluded that, as *Du gamla, du fria* had already become de facto the national anthem, formal recognition was unnecessary. The committee argued that no official confirmation was required, as the anthem's status was already entrenched in Swedish society.[[11]](#cite_note-:2-13)

### National minority languages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Du_gamla,_du_fria&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: National minority languages")]

*Du gamla, du fria* is sung in Swedish in official contexts,[[43]](#cite_note-49) however translations of the lyrics into some of Sweden's national minority languages exist.[[44]](#cite_note-:6-50)[[45]](#cite_note-:3-51)

#### Finnish

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Du_gamla,_du_fria&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Finnish")]

*Pohjolanmaa* (lit. 'Nordic land') is the *de facto* [Finnish](/wiki/Finnish_language "Finnish language") version of the song. Originally translated by Finnish writer [Alpo Noponen](/w/index.php?title=Alpo_Noponen&action=edit&redlink=1 "Alpo Noponen (page does not exist)") [[fi](https://fi.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alpo_Noponen "fi:Alpo Noponen")] (1862–1927),[[46]](#cite_note-Pohjolanmaa-52) it has been used in textbooks produced by the [Swedish National Agency for Education](/wiki/National_Agency_for_Education_(Sweden) "National Agency for Education (Sweden)").[[44]](#cite_note-:6-50) Finnish is an official [*national* *minority language*](/wiki/Languages_of_Sweden#Recognised_minority_languages "Languages of Sweden") in Sweden.[[47]](#cite_note-:5-53)
